title: Quentin Tarantino interview: 'I'm shutting your butt down!'

markers.name: false_analogy
markers.excerpt: there was two holocausts in America... the holocaustic aspects, the Auschwitzian aspects of the slave trade
markers.explanation: Tarantino draws a direct equivalence between slavery and the Holocaust/Auschwitz. While both involve mass atrocity, conflating distinct historical events as analogous can be a rhetorical false analogy used to dramatize a point.

markers.name: loaded_language
markers.excerpt: the holocaustic aspects, the Auschwitzian aspects of the slave trade in America
markers.explanation: Using emotionally charged terms like 'holocaustic' and 'Auschwitzian' to frame the subject matter loads the discussion with strong affective weight beyond descriptive accuracy.

markers.name: red_herring
markers.excerpt: you go and you watch a Kung-Fu movie and one guy takes on a hundred people in a restaurant
markers.explanation: When asked why non-violent people enjoy movie violence, Tarantino pivots to a kung-fu movie example rather than addressing the psychological question, deflecting from the substance of the inquiry.

markers.name: false_analogy
markers.excerpt: filmmaking directors are like boxers. They have their time.
markers.explanation: Tarantino compares directors to boxers to argue both decline with age. While evocative, the analogy equates physical athletic decline with creative/artistic decline, which are not obviously comparable domains.

markers.name: false_analogy
markers.excerpt: it's like asking Judd Apatow why do you like making comedies
markers.explanation: Tarantino deflects a substantive question about his preference for violence by comparing it to asking a comedy director about comedy, treating the two as equivalent when violence raises distinct ethical questions comedy does not.

markers.name: circular_reasoning
markers.excerpt: I think it's good cinema I consider a good cinema
markers.explanation: He justifies making violent films by asserting it's 'good cinema' because he considers it good cinema — restating the conclusion as its own premise.

markers.name: loaded_language
markers.excerpt: there was two Holocaust in America... the Auschwitz nyan house aspects of the slave trade
markers.explanation: Invoking 'Holocaust' and 'Auschwitz' is emotionally charged framing designed to elevate the moral weight of his film's subject matter and preempt criticism of its violence.

markers.name: red_herring
markers.excerpt: there's two types of violence in this movie there's the there's the brutality... put upon the slaves
markers.explanation: Asked why he personally enjoys making violent movies, he pivots to distinguishing types of violence in the film and to historical slavery, diverting from the personal question posed.

markers.name: false_urgency
markers.explanation: Tarantino's refusal framing (referenced in title and continued here with 'I am refusing') applies a forceful conversational shutdown rather than engaging with the substantive question, a rhetorical move to end discussion.

markers.name: red_herring
markers.excerpt: do you want me to do what I've already done before and I am refusing
markers.explanation: Rather than addressing the interviewer's question about serious themes, Tarantino deflects by reframing the question as a demand to repeat himself, diverting from the actual topic.

markers.name: false_analogy
markers.excerpt: filmmaking doctors are like boxers they have the time they have the time
markers.explanation: Comparing filmmakers to boxers (whose decline is physical) is a loose analogy; creative decline and athletic decline operate on different mechanisms, making the comparison rhetorically vivid but logically weak.

markers.name: false_analogy
markers.excerpt: it's like asking Judd Apatow, why do you like making comedies?
markers.explanation: Tarantino equates questions about depicting graphic violence in a slavery film with asking a comedy director why he likes comedy. The moral and artistic stakes differ substantially, making the analogy misleading.

markers.name: loaded_language
markers.excerpt: the holocaustic aspects, the Auschwitzian aspects of the slave trade in America
markers.explanation: Invoking 'holocaust' and 'Auschwitz' is emotionally charged framing designed to elevate the moral weight of his film's subject matter rather than make an argument.

markers.name: proof_by_assertion
markers.excerpt: I think it's good cinema. I consider it good cinema.
markers.explanation: Rather than supporting the claim, Tarantino simply restates that it is good cinema, asserting the conclusion in place of justifying it.

markers.name: special_pleading
markers.excerpt: That hasn't been dealt with in America to the extent that I deal with it
markers.explanation: He carves out a unique justification for his own treatment of the subject, implying his work is exempt from criticism because no one else has tackled it as he has.

markers.name: false_analogy
markers.excerpt: directors are like boxers. They have their time.
markers.explanation: Tarantino compares film directors to boxers to support the claim that directors decline with age. The analogy is rhetorically vivid but logically weak — the skills, physical demands, and career arcs of boxers differ substantially from those of directors.
